subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, coherent description of Dual Coding Theory and Picture Superiority Effect
Explicit aim and educational relevance linked to classroom practice
Wellâoperationalized hypotheses with both IV and DV clearly defined
weaknesses: Rationale for selecting fungi stimuli could be introduced earlier for clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction demonstrates strong theoretical grounding, a clearly stated and wellârelevant aim, and precise hypotheses, satisfying much of the 5â6 band. However, incomplete citation details and a slightly delayed rationale for stimulus choice prevent it from fully meeting all topâband requirements, warranting a score of 4/6.

strengths: Independentâmeasures design is not only described but justified
Volunteer sampling technique and participant criteria are clearly explained
Controlled variables and material choices are listed with rationale
weaknesses: No mention of blinding or steps to prevent experimenter bias
Lacks detail on how consistency of controlled variables was verified
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section meets the explanatory threshold for research design, sampling, and materials (3â4 band). Minor omissionsâsuch as the absence of blinding procedures and verification of variable consistencyâsuggest it does not fully satisfy all topâband descriptors, so 3/4 is appropriate.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive statistics and a justified nonâparametric test
Findings are clearly interpreted in relation to the hypothesis and theory
weaknesses: Graph lacks error bars and full axis labels
Statistical report omits sample size (n), U statistic, and effect size
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analyses are correctly applied and interpretations well linked to theory (5â6 band), but presentation errors in the graph and incomplete reporting of key statistics keep it in the upper midâband. A score of 4/6 reflects strong analysis with room for improvement in completeness and conventions.

strengths: Discussion ties findings back to Dual Coding Theory and prior research
Strengths and limitations are clearly stated and relevant
Modifications are explicitly linked to limitations and justified
weaknesses: External validity and generalizability discussion could be deepened
Some modification suggestions lack detail (e.g., pilotâtesting complexity)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation integrates theory, outlines salient strengths/limitations, and proposes justified modifications, aligning with many topâband descriptors. However, it stops short of fully examining external validity and expanding on certain modifications, placing it in the 3â4 band with a score of 4/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph that directly compares means for the two groups is provided, correctly addressing the hypothesis. Minor conventions are missing (error bars, precise axis labelling), hence small errors remain; consequently the work fits best in the 3-4 band.

The student explicitly links statistical outcomes to the hypothesis (rejecting null, supporting research hypothesis) and goes further to relate the findings back to Paivioâs theory. Interpretation is coherent and data-based, satisfying the 5-6 band.

Both null and research hypotheses are clearly written; the IV (type of presentation: image-text vs text-text) and DV (number of facts correctly recalled) are explicitly named and operationalised (five facts per fungus, counted after 2Â½ min). This satisfies all top-band requirements.

Volunteer sampling is identified and the rationale (practicality, motivation, language control, exclusion of psychology students) is explained, showing an understanding of why this approach was chosen. This meets the explanatory threshold of the 3-4 band.

Dual Coding Theory and the Picture Superiority Effect are described in sufficient depth (two channels, associative processes, previous empirical support) and the student explicitly connects these ideas to the present manipulation (image-text should enhance recall over text-text). The connection is coherent and error-free, warranting placement in the 5â6 band.

Findings are not merely restated but actively discussed in relation to Dual Coding Theory, the Picture Superiority Effect, and prior study (Bablekou et al.). This depth of theoretical integration aligns with the highest band.

The student states a clear, focused aim â comparing image-text with text-text presentation on factual recall â and then explicitly explains why this is educationally relevant (optimising learning strategies, testing generalisability of the Picture Superiority Effect). The explanation moves beyond a mere identification of relevance, linking the outcome to classroom practice, so the work fits solidly in the top band.

Multiple relevant strengths (independent measures, controlled environment, ecological validity) and limitations (self-selection bias, simplicity of material, time constraint) are clearly explained, demonstrating insight into how each point affects validity. This meets top-band descriptors.

Controlled variables (lighting, distractions, timing) and materials (mushroom images, scientific names, simple fonts) are listed and their selection is briefly justified (avoid prior knowledge, linguistic bias). That constitutes explanation, fulfilling the 3-4 band.

Each identified limitation is followed by a concrete, justified modification (increase writing time, employ random sampling, introduce more complex material) directly linked to the stated weakness. The explicit linkage and justification satisfy the 5-6 band.

The independent-measures design is not only described but also justified (avoids consolidation, protects internal validity). This constitutes an explanation rather than a description, so the strand meets the 3-4 band; no deeper methodological evaluation beyond this is offered, so a 4 is appropriate.

Appropriate descriptive statistics (mean, SD) and an appropriate non-parametric inferential test (Mann-Whitney U) are used, with a reported p-value and correct interpretation. No computational inaccuracies are evident from the presentation, so the application appears accurate, meriting the top band.

annotations.comment: The student provides a clear definition of encoding and retrieval, establishing context effectively, but the citation âMcDermott & Roediger, n.d.â lacks a publication date and complete source details. Ensure all references include full bibliographic information.
The description of Dual Coding Theory and the Picture Superiority Effect is well articulated with key concepts, demonstrating solid theoretical grounding.
The transition from the Bablekou et al. study to the current investigation is clear, but the rationale for selecting fungi stimuli could be made more explicit here rather than later.
The student explicitly states the aim and explains its educational relevance with reference to teaching methods, satisfying the top band for aim and relevance.

The explanation of the independent-measures design includes justification for avoiding consolidation, but a discussion of potential order effects or need for counterbalancing could deepen the design critique.
Hypotheses are clearly stated with both null and research versions, and the IV and DV are named and operationalized, showing precise experimental planning.
Descriptive statistics (mean and SD) are presented succinctly, but the table omits sample size (n), which is essential for readers to interpret variability.
Volunteer sampling is described with participant criteria and rationale, demonstrating an understanding of sampling implications.
Controlled variables such as lighting and timing are listed with justification, but the student could explain how they verified consistency across sessions.
The procedural description is replicable and includes ethical considerations, but there is no mention of blinding or steps to avoid experimenter bias.
The textual interpretation links means to the hypothesis coherently, but including an effect size would strengthen the inferential analysis.
The bar graph is clear and directly addresses the hypothesis, but it lacks axis labels and error bars to convey data variability accurately.
The choice of the MannâWhitney U test is well justified given small sample and distribution concerns, demonstrating appropriate inferential planning.
The statistical report includes p = 0.0007 and z but omits the U statistic itself and effect size; include these for completeness.
Linking results back to Dual Coding Theory and educational implications demonstrates strong interpretation and theoretical integration.
The student discusses consistency with Bablekou et al. (2003) and theory, clearly meeting the highest band for findings discussion.
Strengths are well linked to internal validity and ecological validity, showcasing sophisticated evaluation.
The limitation of self-selection bias is identified, but the discussion could be strengthened by considering its impact on external validity and generalizability.
The rationale for using fungi and scientific names to control prior knowledge is clear and demonstrates thoughtful control of confounds.
The proposed modification to increase writing time directly addresses the time constraint limitation; consider also gathering handwriting speed data to tailor timing.
The suggestion to employ random sampling is well linked to the self-selection bias limitation, satisfying the top band for modifications.
Introducing more complex material is a justified modification for mundane realism, but also discuss pilot testing complexity to avoid overload.
The conclusion correctly reports significance at p â¤ 0.05, but the student should specify that the alpha level was determined a priori.
The Bablekou reference is formatted inconsistently; ensure journal title, volume, issue, and DOI/URL follow IB style guidelines.
The McDermott & Roediger reference entry lacks complete URL and date accessed formatting; standardize all references for consistency.
